## [00:00 - 03:02]

So let's talk about ethical bribes and how you can discover ways that you can ethically bribe your contacts to get referrals. In this section, we're going to talk about eight different ethical types of bribes that you can provide to get referrals from your contacts. We're going to talk about how you can actually incorporate those eight ideas into your business, and we're going to talk about how you can leverage our tool, GG, to help you with that. But before we do that, let's talk about can we even afford to bribe our customers? What's the math behind bribing our customers, and how do we actually figure out if we can afford to really make it worth their while for us to do that? In order to do that, I'm going to take you over to the whiteboard real quick to show you some of the math behind the actual referral side of things. You may recall from Dean's previous training about the value ladder, just to amount to value there actually is in your customers when you build out the entire value ladder. But if we have $250,000 worth of value, and we divide that by the hundred total customers that came through, we'd have an average lifetime value per customer of about $2,500 per customer. So if we have a lifetime value, LTV, and that's $2,500, then we know that for every hundred customers we're going to get roughly $2,500 per customer. But then there's another thing we have to consider, and that's the actual profit that we want to make. So let's say for each customer, you want to make $1,000 profit. We subtract the $1,000 from the lifetime value of $2,500, you have $1,500 left. Pretty simple math so far. Then you have your actual cost of doing business. Overhead, that might include an assistant, that might include softwares, that might include various different things within your business, but you're going to have some type of overhead. And let's say on average it's $500. We now have $1,000 left. Now whatever it costs you to actually acquire that customer is what's referred to as cost to acquire your customer, or CAC. The CAC is how much you can actually afford to spend to acquire your customer. So in this case you could afford to spend $1,000 whether that be on referrals or whether that be on paid ads or whether that be paying someone to help you with social media or whatever that might be. You could afford to spend $1,000 in order to get $2,500 for your lifetime value of a

customer which would give you that $1,000 worth of profit.
